title: Transaction Control on Sybase

Transaction Control on Sybase

Transaction Control on Sybase

Both commit and rollback instruction are fully supported by Sybase.

Two-Phase Commit

The Sybase Client-Library product does not support two-phase commit.

Starting Transactions

Unless the logon path is already in a transaction, the connector initiates a transaction for the following operations:

  • Insert
  • Update
  • Delete
  • Lock

When the connector begins, commits, or rolls back a transaction, it checks the Sybase @@trancount variable to determine if the logon path is already in a transaction.

A new transaction is not started if the path is already in a transaction, and commit or rollback is not requested if the path is not in a transaction. This behavior enables the Sybase connector to work with a database that is configured to use chained transactions.

Chained Transactions

Users can configure the Sybase DBMS to run standard Sybase transactions or ANSI-compatible Sybase chained transactions.

CAUTION: When chained transactions are used, the SYB connector implicitly begins a transaction before any data is modified or retrieved, unless the logon path is already in a transaction.

For more information on chained transactions, refer to your Sybase documentation .

Referential Integrity Violations and Transactions

Inserts, updates, or deletes which violate a restricted relation do not cause the logon path’s transaction to be rolled back unless the Uniface write trigger or store trigger is coded to issue a rollback when the store fails. Sybase only rolls back the command which caused the referential integrity violation and not the entire transaction.

Isolation Level

Sybase allows you to set the default isolation level for locking to the following:

  • Isolation level zero—allows dirty reads. This means the user can read a row which is possibly never stored in the database.
  • Isolation level one—avoids dirty reads which means that a query can only retrieve rows which have been written to the database. A dirty read is a row which has been modified, but not committed to the database.
  • Isolation level three—provides repeatable reads, which means that a row read more than once during a transaction has the same value for both reads.

    Isolation level three is equivalent to Uniface paranoid locking which is used with the read /lock command. A disadvantage of isolation level three is that concurrency is often reduced because locks are held longer. Isolation level one is equivalent to Uniface optimistic locking.

The standard default Sybase locking is isolation level one. The ANSI standard requires that isolation level three be used as the default. The lock level connector option can be set to change the default level. Also, the global variable, @@isolation, indicates the current level of isolation.

For applications which require ANSI-style locking, set the lock level to 3.

Related Links
  • Transaction Control

    A transaction is a complete, logical unit of work. It usually involves adding, deleting or updating one or more logically related records in one or more databases. With Uniface, you can explicitly define a transaction with the commit ProcScript instruction. If an error occurs, a transaction can be rolled back with the rollback ProcScript instruction.
